Werewolves of Denby
A game of life & death · 8–21 players

Rules

Werewolves · by AR · v10.0 FINAL

I

General idea

Townspeople vs. Werewolves, and no one knows who is who. Everyone is randomly assigned a secret role — a Townsperson role or a Werewolf role — and roles are never revealed.

Start with at least 2 Werewolves, 3 basic Townspersons, and 3 unique Special roles. Day and night alternate, with players eliminated each day and night until either the Townspeople kill every Werewolf, or the Werewolves reach parity at the end of any day or night.

II

Night

The game begins at night. Eyes closed. No talking. Open your eyes only when called on. Except for the first night, the Moderator must say the exact same lines every night. The Werewolves must vote to kill one player, and Specials may use their powers. Deaths are announced at the end.

All actions resolve at the end of the night. First night only: the Werewolves “kill” the Moderator to begin the game.

III

Day

Eyes open, no rules — try to find the Werewolves and vote to eliminate one player. Deliberate for 5 minutes, then vote by pointing at another player.

Before the game begins, choose one voting system:

Majority voting: A player is eliminated only if they receive a majority of votes. If no one has a majority, deliberate for 2 more minutes, then re-vote between the top two candidates. Beginner-friendly; Village advantage.

Plurality voting: The player with the most votes is eliminated immediately. Not beginner-friendly; Werewolf advantage.

The Moderator breaks all ties, unless a player is the Mayor.

First day only: when time is called, players raise their hands if ready to vote. Unless a two-thirds majority are ready, a standing trust-vote is held — a random person begins and picks someone to trust; the picked person sits and picks the next, until one player remains, and that player is eliminated.

IV

Balance

The number of Special roles must be within one of the number of Townspeople.

Each role’s +/− value is its relative strength; a balanced game should total between −3 and +3 at the start.

V

Strategy

The Special roles exist to help the Village identify and kill the Wolves. Without specials or teamwork, the Wolves win over 80% of games; with specials and good teamwork, the Village wins over 50%.

VI

The fine print

A game can end five ways. If more than one could apply, check them in this order at the end of each day and night:

  1. The Lovers win — if one Lover is a Wolf, and the two Lovers are the last two players alive.
  2. The Werewolves reach parity — living Wolves equal or outnumber living non-Wolves.
  3. A draw — if no one is left alive, no one wins.
  4. The Vampire wins — at the end of the night only, if the Vampire survives and all other living players are marked.
  5. The Village wins — if every Werewolf is dead.

Nothing is ever revealed. A dead player’s role stays secret, and a claimed role is only ever a claim — the Moderator never confirms it. Every deduction runs on unverified claims.

Three minor exceptions. In the morning, the Moderator will announce if the Oracle is alive. At night, after a lynch, the Moderator will announce if the Coroner is alive. At night, the Moderator will announce if the Alpha Wolf uses its Unstoppable attack.

VII

Night order

The sequence the Moderator follows at night. Most steps wake a role; the Coroner step is a closed-eyes public line and tap after a lynch. Everything resolves together at the end of the night; the tags mark which nights a role acts.

1Cupidfirst night only
Picks two Lovers; the Moderator taps them to acknowledge each other.
2Werewolvesfirst night only
Open their eyes and learn who the other Werewolves are.
3Coronerafter a lynch
Learns if the Village lynched a Wolf.
4Bodyguardnot first night
Picks a player to protect.
5Werewolvesnot first night
Kills one player by unanimous agreement.
6Witchnot first night
Shown the Werewolves’ victim (if her save is unused), and may save them and/or kill a player.
7Assassinnot first night
Learns if the Fortune Teller is dead, if so: kills a player or learns if a player is a Werewolf — once of each for two nights.
8Hunternot first night
Learns if they died this night and, if so, picks a player to kill.
9Detectivenot first night
Picks two new players and learns whether they’re on the same team — until the first “No.”
10Fortune Tellerevery night
Learns whether one player is a Werewolf.
11Vampireevery night
Marks one new player.
Social deduction · the cast

Roles

The Fortune Teller is always in play; every game has at least two basic Werewolves and at least three Townspeople. Numbers are each role’s balance weight. Nothing here is ever revealed in play — all information is claimed.

The VillageTown-aligned. Win by finding and eliminating every Werewolf.

Townsperson

+1

Find the Werewolves. Be awesome. Every game includes at least three.

Fortune Teller

+7

Select a player each night and learn if they are a Werewolf. Always in the game.

Hunter

+4

Kill a player if you die. If the Village kills you, you kill the player you voted for. Win a tied game with one Wolf left.

Witch

+4

Once each per game: save the Wolves’ victim and/or kill a player at night. May save yourself.

Assassin

+3

For the next two nights after the FT dies you must: kill a player, or learn if any player is a Werewolf. Once each.

Detective

+3

Each night, pick two new players and learn if they are on the same team. Stop after the first ‘different’ hit. Lovers are always on the same team. May not pick yourself.

Bodyguard

+2

Pick a player each night. All attempts that night to kill that player kill you instead. The Witch can save you from the Wolves.

  • If you have a Lover, you must protect them every night, even if they are the Mayor.

Tough Guy

+2

Survive the first Wolves’ night-kill attempt. You are not told this happened. This does not protect from the Alpha Wolf, or from any non-Wolf kill.

Oracle

+1

While alive, the village is told publicly at the start of each day whether “More than one Wolf remains” or “Exactly one Wolf remains.”

Coroner

+1

While alive, every night after a lynch, the Moderator announces publicly that “The Coroner checks the body” and taps you three times if the lynched player was a Wolf.

Cupid

−2

The first night, pick two Lovers. If one Lover dies, the other dies immediately. May pick yourself.

  • If one Lover is a Wolf, the Lovers can win by being the last two players alive.
  • If the Bodyguard dies from protecting their Lover, the protected Lover does not die.
  • If one Lover is a Vampire, the other Lover is marked by default, without their knowledge.

Lycan

−2

A Townsperson who appears as a Wolf to the Fortune Teller, Assassin, and Coroner.

The PackWolf-aligned. Win by reaching parity with the village.

Werewolf

−6

The Werewolves learn each other’s identities the first night. Each night thereafter they must agree, unanimously, on one player to kill — any player, including themselves or another Wolf. Every game includes at least two.

Alpha Wolf

−7

Once per game, make the Wolves’ kill unstoppable; the Moderator announces this publicly. Bodyguard and Tough Guy protection fails, and the Witch cannot save the target.

Elder Wolf

−8

Once per game, at night, you may void the next day’s lynch: The vote is held but the condemned player does not die. The Moderator announces that the lynch was voided and night begins.

The VampireIts own faction. Wins by marking the whole table.

Vampire

+0

Every night, mark a new living player. You win if you wake with the town and every other living player is marked. The Vampire counts as a Townsperson for Wolf parity.

  • If the Vampire has a Lover, the Lover is marked by default, without their knowledge.
The OfficeClaimed in play, not dealt at setup.

Mayor

—

Not a dealt role — an office. Once per game, at day start, while five or more players are alive, the first player to claim Mayor gains a double vote for the rest of the game and breaks any ties. Each day, the Mayor casts the first vote, and their selection has a chance to speak before the rest of the players vote. The Mayor cannot be saved or protected at night.

  • If the Mayor’s lover is the Bodyguard, the Mayor can and must be protected by them.
  • Tough Guy Mayor still survives their first Werewolf attack.
Social deduction · simulation engine

Werewolves of Denby

A faithful model of the tabletop game — roster generation, role abilities, whispered intel, claims, coalition dynamics, and full vote resolution. Replay any seed and read the night as it unfolds.

v10.0 15 roles Seeded replay
Game setup
Inspect a single game in full detail, or run a batch to study faction balance.
Seeded & reproducible

Mode

Majority + Runoff requires a majority first, then a top-two plurality if needed. Single Plurality eliminates the top vote-getter on the first final vote.

Roster and balance

If set, overrides the band and targets this exact point total.
Pins the pack size for this run. Alpha/Elder are set separately by the force-include boxes below — e.g. 4 wolves with both checked = 2 plain wolves + Alpha + Elder. Blank restores normal rung sampling.
Force include roles (Fortune Teller is always included)
Generator rules
• Total points must fall within the balance band.
• Always includes FT + at least 3 ordinary Townspeople.
• Basic Townspeople ≤ Special Townspeople + 2.
• Always includes Witch and/or Assassin (prefers Witch-present).
• Wolves: 8–9 → 2; 12+ → at least 3; up to 5 at 21 players.
• Special Wolves require at least 2 basic Wolves: Alpha or Elder can appear only as the 3rd Wolf; both require 4+ Wolves.
• No duplicate specials; duplicates only for basic Wolf/Town.
• Generator can include Alpha and/or Elder Wolf when feasible.
Simulation output
The full timeline, narrated turn by turn. Search and filter by event type; deeper vote diagnostics sit one click away.

Coalition debug

Werewolves of Denby
Created by Andrew Rejent · © 2026
A hand-built variant of the classic Werewolf / Mafia social-deduction game, refined over roughly two decades of play.

Free to use, play, share, and modify for non-commercial purposes, with credit. Please don’t sell it, charge for it, or build anything commercial from it — in whole, in part, or as a derivative. The classic Werewolf / Mafia game itself isn’t claimed; this covers only this specific implementation, its code, original art, and original text. “Werewolves of Denby” is a trademark of Andrew Rejent.
